The scene then transitions to a real Gmail inbox interface, showing a list of emails, including the phishing email, to demonstrate how such scams appear in a user's inbox. The video then shifts to a title card reading 'Understanding Digital Tracking' in white text on a teal background, followed by an animated sequence illustrating digital tracking. This sequence shows a user browsing a fashion website with high-heeled shoes, then transitions to a health article about eggs for breakfast, where an ad for 'DISCOUNT SHOES!!!' appears on the right sidebar, visually demonstrating how online behavior is tracked and used for targeted advertising.

segments.integrated_caption: A clear, informative male voice narrates throughout the segment. The video begins with a desktop browser window displaying a health article titled 'Eggs for Breakfast' alongside a sidebar ad for 'DISCOUNT SHOES!!!' featuring purple high-heeled shoes, which the narrator identifies as 'an ad for shoes.' The scene transitions to a different ad on the same page, now showing a purple shoe with the slogan 'New shoe. New you.', and a video ad for 'Adorable kitten tries to wear hat!' with a countdown to skip, as the narrator explains, 'You switch over to YouTube to watch a hilarious kitten video, but first you have to watch a shoe ad.' Next, a smartphone screen appears, showing a sponsored ad for 'Shoe Discounts' with 'HALF OFF ALL SHOES!!!!' and images of red shoes, with the narrator stating, 'Later, you open the Facebook app on your phone, and guess what? Sponsored post about a shoe sale.' The three screens (desktop with egg article, smartphone with shoe discount, and desktop with shoe ad) are then shown side-by-side, prompting the narrator to ask, 'How do all of these sites know that you're shopping for shoes?' A burst of colorful geometric shapes (circles, squares) animates and clusters, then transforms into a list of keywords like 'nutrition', 'food - eggs', 'women’s shoes', 'cats, kittens', and 'Italian food', expanding into a full list of interests. The narrator explains that 'websites use various methods to track your browsing habits' and that 'together, it forms a sort of digital profile that advertisers can use to learn all kinds of things about you.' The scene shifts to a minimalist blue background with a laptop, where a document icon appears and is pinned to the screen, followed by an 'AD' placeholder, illustrating the narrator's explanation that 'An advertiser can plant a small file on your computer called a tracking cookie, which they can use to identify you the next time you visit one of the sites they advertise on.' The laptop then displays a social media interface, and a smartphone appears beside it, connected by a dotted line, indicating data synchronization. The narrator elaborates that advertisers 'can also get a pretty good idea of which devices you use based on your social media and email accounts, or by using a technique called probabilistic matching, which makes educated guesses about which devices belong to the same person.' Finally, a central hexagonal icon of a stylized man with blue hair and an orange shirt appears, connected by dotted lines to three devices (laptop, tablet, smartphone) displaying different content (health article, colorful blocks, pink ad), with matching percentages (46%, 32%, 69%) that rapidly increase to 97%, 94%, and 95%, visually illustrating the high degree of user interest matching across devices, as the narrator concludes, 'So, one way or another, all of your devices are...'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a blue background showcasing a laptop, tablet, and smartphone, all displaying a 'Eggs for Breakfast' health article. A pop-up ad for 'HUGE SALE ON ALL SHOES!!' suddenly appears on all three screens, visually demonstrating how companies send targeted ads across devices, as the narrator explains this information is "targeted to you." The screens then transition to show a large, stylized eye on each device, symbolizing digital tracking, with the tablet's eye zooming in to reveal a colorful mosaic of geometric shapes, representing data collection. The narrator highlights that this raises "privacy concerns, since the information is collected without your knowledge." The scene shifts to a laptop where 'How to juggle fire' is typed into a search bar, followed by a hand punching a document labeled 'Insurance Coverage,' illustrating the narrator's question: "could an insurance company deny coverage based on a person's browsing history?" The background changes to beige as colorful geometric shapes float across the screen, converging into a blue shield labeled 'Block Tracking Cookies,' emphasizing steps to "limit the amount of data collected, such as blocking tracking cookies." The laptop screen then displays a 'Health' webpage with 'Recommended Articles,' followed by 'How to cook an egg' typed into a search bar, leading to a data visualization dashboard filled with graphs and charts. The narrator explains these are "potential upsides" allowing sites to "recommend articles and videos based on your interests." The final frame shows a multi-layered, colorful rectangular frame with the text 'MODERN ONLINE REALITY,' summarizing the theme as the narrator concludes that "digital tracking is part of the modern online reality."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a retro-styled title card displaying 'MODERN ONLINE REALITY' against a beige background, followed by a solid teal screen with 'Windows Basics' in white. A female narrator begins, stating, "If you're just getting started with Windows, learning how to navigate the interface is a great place to start." The scene then shifts to a Windows 8.1 desktop with a blue geometric wallpaper, showing various icons and a taskbar. The narrator continues, "Let's take a look at the desktop, which is the screen you see here. It includes a desktop background, also known as your wallpaper, and the taskbar at the bottom of the screen." A red border briefly appears around the Windows 8.1 desktop before cutting to a Windows 7 desktop with its classic blue sky wallpaper. The narrator explains, "The taskbar is where you'll find shortcuts to some of the applications on your computer, as well as the Start button." The Start menu, listing programs like Microsoft Office and Notepad, opens on the Windows 7 desktop as the narrator adds, "In most versions of Windows, you can click the Start button to see a list of applications, files, and settings." The video returns to the Windows 8.1 desktop, then transitions to the Windows 8 Start screen with its colorful live tiles. The narrator clarifies, "But if you're using Windows 8, you can click the Start button to return to the Start screen." The scene reverts to the Windows 8.1 desktop, and a File Explorer window titled 'This PC' opens, displaying folders and drives. The narrator identifies this, saying, "The taskbar is also where you'll find File Explorer, which allows you to view and open files and folders." She concludes, "For now, I'm going to close the window by clicking the X," as the File Explorer window closes, returning to the desktop view.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with a title card displaying 'MODERN ONLINE REALITY' in a retro-styled, multi-layered rectangular frame with pastel-colored borders, set against a beige background. This transitions to a solid teal screen with the text 'Windows Basics' in white. The scene then shifts to a Windows 8.1 desktop with a blue geometric wallpaper, showing icons for Recycle Bin, Google Chrome, Deeznp, family photos, Parallels Shared Folders, and Resume, along with the taskbar at the bottom. The desktop is shown again with a red border briefly appearing around the screen, possibly indicating a focus or selection. The video then cuts to a Windows 7 desktop with the classic blue sky and Windows logo wallpaper, featuring icons for Recycle Bin, Letters, Resume, Brochures, and Twitter, and the Start menu is opened to reveal programs like Microsoft Office, Calculator, and Notepad. The scene returns to the Windows 8.1 desktop, then transitions to the Windows 8 Start screen with colorful live tiles for Mail, Calendar, Internet Explorer, Store, and others. The video returns to the Windows 8.1 desktop, and a File Explorer window titled 'This PC' is opened, displaying folders like Desktop, Documents, Downloads, Music, Pictures, and Videos, along with devices and drives. The window is then closed, returning to the desktop view.

segments.integrated_caption: A female instructional voice begins by stating, "Let's check out more features on the desktop instead," and explains that "The desktop is the main workspace for your computer." As she instructs, "To open a program, file or folder, just double-click the icon," the video shows a Windows 7 desktop with a blue geometric wallpaper and various icons. The user double-clicks the 'Resume' icon, opening a Microsoft Word document titled 'Resume - Word' for 'Olenna Mason'. The voice continues, "Each time you want to open something, it will appear in a new window." The user then opens the 'family photos' folder via File Explorer, revealing image files and a 'vacation' folder. The narrator explains, "You have the ability to move windows by clicking and dragging the top of the window," and "When you're done, just release the mouse," as the user resizes the File Explorer window to view both the resume and photos side-by-side. The voice further advises, "If you have more than one window open at a time, you can quickly switch between them by clicking the icon for that window on the taskbar," as the user minimizes the Word document, leaving only the File Explorer window open. The narrator then describes maximizing and returning windows to original size, before concluding, "When you're finished, closing the window is easy. Just click the X," at which point the user closes the File Explorer window, returning to the desktop. The taskbar displays the time as 1:47 PM, updating to 1:48 PM, with the date 9/25/2014 and system language 'ENG'. The segment ends with the voice stating, "That covers the basics. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens briefly on a Windows 7 desktop interface, displaying a blue geometric wallpaper and various icons, before transitioning to a macOS desktop, which becomes the primary visual focus. The cursor then hovers over the Finder icon, displaying a 'Finder' tooltip, and the narrator instructs, "To open any of the items on your dock, just click the icon." The narrator adds, "There are actually several different ways that you can navigate to files, folders, and apps in OS 10. The Finder feature is one of them." For now, I'm going to close the window by clicking the red button in the upper left corner," as the Finder window remains open, showcasing the file structure against the visible desktop background and dock.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with a close-up of a macOS Maps application displaying a detailed map of Raleigh, North Carolina, with a blue dot marking the user's current location. A female voice explains that entering full-screen mode, which the user activates with the keyboard shortcut Control+Command+F, "causes the window to expand, so you can focus on the application without any other distractions." The desktop background, a blue pattern of concentric circles, becomes visible around the edges of the full-screen map. The user then exits full-screen mode by pressing Control+Command+F, as instructed by the narrator, returning to the standard windowed view. The narrator adds that "not all apps support this feature, but it's available in several of the apps that come pre-installed on your computer." The scene then shifts as the narrator introduces the topic of "using your mouse in OS X," specifically "a feature called natural scrolling." The user clicks the Apple menu in the top-left corner, revealing a dropdown, and selects 'System Preferences...', which opens a new window. The narrator explains, "You can learn more about how it works by going to System Preferences," and then instructs to choose "Mouse or Trackpad, depending on what you have. In this case, we'll choose Mouse." The user clicks on the 'Mouse' icon, opening the Mouse preferences pane. This pane shows options for 'Scroll direction: natural', 'Secondary click' (set to 'Click on right side'), and 'Smart zoom', along with a tracking slider and a visual representation of a hand using a white mouse. The narrator explains, "As you can see from the demo, natural scrolling is basically the reverse of traditional scrolling. It's almost like you're pushing the content up and down on the page." The user interacts with the 'Secondary click' dropdown, and the visual demonstration updates. The user then moves the tracking slider slightly to the right, increasing the mouse speed, while the visual demonstration continues to show the hand moving the mouse. The narrator concludes that while this "may seem awkward at first, but it's actually how many touchscreen devices work." The mouse battery level is displayed as 45%. The entire sequence takes place on a Mac desktop with a blue wave-patterned background and a dock at the bottom.

The user types 'www.weather.com', navigating to The Weather Channel's website, which shows the current weather for Raleigh, NC as 89°F and partly cloudy. After viewing the weather, the user returns to the DOCKSMITH shop tab. The narrator then advises, "If you're on a website that you know you'll want to visit again later... you can bookmark it.
